examples.title: Algorithm: GPIO Access via Linux sysfs
examples.description: Demonstrates the sequence of operations to control GPIO pins on Raspberry Pi through the Linux sysfs interface
examples.code:
ALGORITHM GPIO_Control
INPUT: pin_number (GPIO pin to control), state (HIGH or LOW)
OUTPUT: Physical pin voltage change

BEGIN
  # Step 1: Export the GPIO pin
  IF /sys/class/gpio/gpio{pin_number} does NOT exist THEN
    WRITE pin_number TO /sys/class/gpio/export
    WAIT 100ms for pin to be created
  END IF

  # Step 2: Set pin direction to output
  WRITE "out" TO /sys/class/gpio/gpio{pin_number}/direction

  # Step 3: Set the pin state
  IF state = HIGH THEN
    WRITE "1" TO /sys/class/gpio/gpio{pin_number}/value
  ELSE
    WRITE "0" TO /sys/class/gpio/gpio{pin_number}/value
  END IF

  # Step 4: Verify the state (optional readback)
  READ value FROM /sys/class/gpio/gpio{pin_number}/value

  # Step 5: Clean up when done (unexport)
  WRITE pin_number TO /sys/class/gpio/unexport

END

ALGORITHM Monitor_System_Resources
INPUT: None
OUTPUT: Display of CPU, Memory, Disk, Temperature

BEGIN
  # Get CPU usage
  EXECUTE "top -bn1" command and parse CPU line
  
  # Get Memory usage  
  EXECUTE "free -h" command and parse output
  
  # Get Disk usage
  EXECUTE "df -h" command and parse output
  
  # Get Raspberry Pi temperature
  EXECUTE "vcgencmd measure_temp" command
  
  DISPLAY all collected metrics
END
examples.explanation: The sysfs GPIO interface provides a way to interact with hardware pins through the Linux filesystem. The algorithm shows the essential steps: export creates the pin interface, direction sets input/output mode, value writes the actual voltage state. This approach works without special drivers and is fundamental to Linux-based IoT projects.

examples.title: Python: GPIO Control with gpiozero Library
examples.description: Demonstrates controlling Raspberry Pi GPIO pins using the beginner-friendly gpiozero library for IoT applications
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Raspberry Pi GPIO Control using gpiozero Library
This example demonstrates basic LED control and button input
for IoT applications on Raspberry Pi
"""

from gpiozero import LED, Button, PWMLED
from signal import pause
import time

# Configuration: GPIO pin numbers (BCM numbering)
LED_PIN = 17          # Physical pin 11, GPIO17
BUTTON_PIN = 18       # Physical pin 12, GPIO18
PWM_LED_PIN = 27      # Physical pin 13, GPIO27

def basic_led_control():
    """Basic LED on/off control"""
    led = LED(LED_PIN)
    
    # Turn LED on
    led.on()
    time.sleep(1)
    
    # Turn LED off
    led.off()
    time.sleep(1)
    
    # Toggle LED
    led.toggle()
    time.sleep(1)

def pwm_led_control():
    """Pulse Width Modulation for dimming effect"""
    pwm_led = PWMLED(PWM_LED_PIN)
    
    # Gradually increase brightness
    for brightness in range(0, 101, 10):
        pwm_led.value = brightness / 100.0
        time.sleep(0.1)
    
    # Gradually decrease brightness
    for brightness in range(100, -1, -10):
        pwm_led.value = brightness / 100.0
        time.sleep(0.1)

def button_led_control():
    """Control LED with button press"""
    led = LED(LED_PIN)
    button = Button(BUTTON_PIN, pull_up=True)
    
    # When button is pressed, toggle LED
    button.when_pressed = led.toggle
    
    # Keep script running
    pause()

def blink_led(duration=5, interval=0.5):
    """Blink LED for specified duration"""
    led = LED(LED_PIN)
    end_time = time.time() + duration
    
    while time.time() < end_time:
        led.on()
        time.sleep(interval)
        led.off()
        time.sleep(interval)

# Example: Create an IoT sensor class
class IoTSensor:
    """Simulates an IoT sensor with LED indicator"""
    
    def __init__(self, led_pin=LED_PIN, sensor_id="SENSOR_001"):
        self.led = LED(led_pin)
        self.sensor_id = sensor_id
        self.is_active = False
    
    def activate(self):
        """Activate sensor and show LED"""
        self.led.on()
        self.is_active = True
        print(f"{self.sensor_id}: Activated")
    
    def deactivate(self):
        """Deactivate sensor and turn off LED"""
        self.led.off()
        self.is_active = False
        print(f"{self.sensor_id}: Deactivated")
    
    def read_data(self):
        """Simulate reading sensor data"""
        import random
        if self.is_active:
            return {
                "sensor_id": self.sensor_id,
                "temperature": round(random.uniform(20.0, 30.0), 2),
                "humidity": round(random.uniform(40.0, 60.0), 2)
            }
        return None

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Raspberry Pi GPIO Control Example")
    print("=" * 40)
    
    # Test basic LED control
    print("\n1. Testing basic LED control...")
    basic_led_control()
    
    # Test PWM dimming
    print("\n2. Testing PWM LED dimming...")
    pwm_led_control()
    
    # Test IoT sensor class
    print("\n3. Testing IoT Sensor class...")
    sensor = IoTSensor(sensor_id="TEMP_001")
    sensor.activate()
    data = sensor.read_data()
    print(f"Sensor data: {data}")
    sensor.deactivate()
    
    # Test blink function
    print("\n4. Testing LED blink for 3 seconds...")
    blink_led(duration=3, interval=0.3)
    
    print("\nGPIO Control Example Complete!")
examples.explanation: The gpiozero library provides a high-level, Pythonic interface to Raspberry Pi GPIO pins. This example shows basic LED control (on/off), PWM dimming for variable brightness, button input handling, and a practical IoT sensor class. The library handles all the low-level sysfs interactions automatically, making it ideal for IoT prototyping.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n iterations of LED operations

examples.title: C: Direct GPIO Access via sysfs
examples.description: Demonstrates low-level GPIO control on Raspberry Pi using direct sysfs file operations in C
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates direct low-level control of Raspberry Pi GPIO pins through the Linux sysfs interface. It uses standard file operations (open, write, read, close) to interact with virtual files in /sys/class/gpio/. The code shows how to export/unexport pins, set direction (input/output), and read/write values. This approach is fundamental to understanding how Linux abstracts hardware access and is essential for writing efficient IoT applications.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n blink iterations, O(1) for single read/write operations

examples.title: Python: Systemd Service Management for IoT
examples.description: Demonstrates creating systemd service files and managing services for auto-starting IoT applications on Raspberry Pi boot
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Systemd Service Management for Raspberry Pi IoT Applications
This module demonstrates creating and managing systemd services
for automated IoT application startup on Raspberry Pi
"""

import os
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Service file template for IoT applications
SERVICE_TEMPLATE = """[Unit]
Description={description}
After=network.target
Wants=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User={user}
WorkingDirectory={working_dir}
ExecStart={exec_start}
Restart=always
RestartSec={restart_sec}
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
"""

class SystemdServiceManager:
    """Manager class for systemd services on Raspberry Pi"""
    
    def __init__(self, service_name, config=None):
        self.service_name = service_name
        self.config = config or {}
        self.service_file_path = f"/etc/systemd/system/{service_name}.service"
    
    def create_service_file(self, description, exec_start, user="pi", 
                           working_dir="/home/pi", restart_sec=10):
        """Create a systemd service file"""
        service_content = SERVICE_TEMPLATE.format(
            description=description,
            user=user,
            working_dir=working_dir,
            exec_start=exec_start,
            restart_sec=restart_sec
        )
        
        with open(self.service_file_path, 'w') as f:
            f.write(service_content)
        
        os.chmod(self.service_file_path, 0o644)
        print(f"Service file created: {self.service_file_path}")
        return True
    
    def run_command(self, cmd):
        """Run a shell command with sudo"""
        full_cmd = f"sudo {cmd}"
        result = subprocess.run(
            full_cmd, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True
        )
        return result.returncode, result.stdout, result.stderr
    
    def daemon_reload(self):
        """Reload systemd daemon"""
        print("Reloading systemd daemon...")
        code, out, err = self.run_command("systemctl daemon-reload")
        if code != 0:
            print(f"Error: {err}")
        return code == 0
    
    def enable(self):
        """Enable service to start at boot"""
        print(f"Enabling service: {self.service_name}")
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl enable {self.service_name}")
        if code == 0:
            print("Service enabled successfully")
        else:
            print(f"Error enabling: {err}")
        return code == 0
    
    def disable(self):
        """Disable service from starting at boot"""
        print(f"Disabling service: {self.service_name}")
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl disable {self.service_name}")
        return code == 0
    
    def start(self):
        """Start the service"""
        print(f"Starting service: {self.service_name}")
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl start {self.service_name}")
        if code == 0:
            print("Service started successfully")
        else:
            print(f"Error: {err}")
        return code == 0
    
    def stop(self):
        """Stop the service"""
        print(f"Stopping service: {self.service_name}")
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl stop {self.service_name}")
        return code == 0
    
    def restart(self):
        """Restart the service"""
        print(f"Restarting service: {self.service_name}")
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl restart {self.service_name}")
        return code == 0
    
    def status(self):
        """Get service status"""
        code, out, err = self.run_command(f"systemctl status {self.service_name}")
        print(out)
        return code == 0
    
    def get_logs(self, lines=50):
        """Get service journal logs"""
        code, out, err = self.run_command(
            f"journalctl -u {self.service_name} -n {lines} --no-pager"
        )
        print(out)
        return out
    
    def uninstall(self):
        """Remove the service completely"""
        print(f"Uninstalling service: {self.service_name}")
        self.stop()
        self.disable()
        
        if os.path.exists(self.service_file_path):
            os.remove(self.service_file_path)
            self.daemon_reload()
            print("Service removed successfully")
        return True


def create_sample_iot_app():
    """Create a sample IoT sensor application"""
    app_dir = Path("/home/pi/iot_projects")
    app_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    
    app_content = '''#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Sample IoT Sensor Application for Raspberry Pi"""

import os
import time
import json
import logging
from datetime import datetime

logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.INFO,
    format="%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s"
)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def read_temperature():
    import random
    return round(20.0 + random.uniform(0, 10.0), 2)

def main():
    sensor_mode = os.environ.get("SENSOR_MODE", "test")
    logger.info(f"Starting IoT Sensor in {sensor_mode} mode")
    
    reading_count = 0
    
    try:
        while True:
            reading_count += 1
            temperature = read_temperature()
            
            data = {
                "sensor_id": "TEMP_001",
                "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
                "temperature": temperature,
                "unit": "celsius"
            }
            
            logger.info(f"Reading {reading_count}: {json.dumps(data)}")
            time.sleep(60)
            
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        logger.info("Sensor app stopped by user")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
'''
    
    app_path = app_dir / "sensor_app.py"
    with open(app_path, 'w') as f:
        f.write(app_content)
    
    app_path.chmod(0o755)
    print(f"Sample IoT app created at: {app_path}")
    return str(app_path)


def main():
    """Main function demonstrating systemd service management"""
    print("Raspberry Pi IoT Service Management Demo")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Step 1: Create sample IoT application
    print("\n[Step 1] Creating sample IoT application...")
    app_path = create_sample_iot_app()
    
    # Step 2: Create service manager
    print("\n[Step 2] Creating service configuration...")
    manager = SystemdServiceManager("iot-sensor")
    
    # Step 3: Create and install service file
    print("\n[Step 3] Installing systemd service...")
    manager.create_service_file(
        description="IoT Temperature Sensor Service",
        exec_start=f"/usr/bin/python3 {app_path}",
        user="pi",
        working_dir="/home/pi/iot_projects",
        restart_sec=10
    )
    
    # Step 4: Reload and enable service
    print("\n[Step 4] Enabling service...")
    manager.daemon_reload()
    manager.enable()
    
    # Step 5: Start service
    print("\n[Step 5] Starting service...")
    manager.start()
    
    # Give it a moment to start
    time.sleep(2)
    
    # Step 6: Check status
    print("\n[Step 6] Checking service status...")
    manager.status()
    
    # Step 7: View logs
    print("\n[Step 7] Viewing service logs...")
    manager.get_logs(10)
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 50)
    print("Demo complete! Service is now installed and running.")
    print("To check status: sudo systemctl status iot-sensor")
    print("To stop: sudo systemctl stop iot-sensor")
    print("To uninstall: Run manager.uninstall()")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Check if running as root for installation
    if os.geteuid() == 0:
        print("Warning: Running as root. Some operations may require sudo.")
    
    main()
examples.explanation: This Python module demonstrates creating and managing systemd services for IoT applications on Raspberry Pi. It includes a SystemdServiceManager class that handles service file creation, enabling/disabling services, starting/stopping services, checking status, and viewing logs with journalctl. The example shows how to create a sample IoT sensor application and register it as a systemd service that automatically starts at boot—essential for deploying production IoT applications that must run continuously.
